title: Method Declaration and Calling
description: What a method is, how to declare one, and how to call it — Java's version of a function.
objectives: Understand why we group code into methods
Declare a method with a return type, name, and parameter list
Call a method and understand what happens when it runs

sections.text: We've written all our code so far directly inside main(). If we needed to calculate a square twice, we'd have to repeat the same lines. A method lets us name a block of code once and reuse it wherever needed.
A named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task, which can be called (invoked) from other places in a program.
You've actually already called methods without naming the concept: System.out.println(...) and sc.nextInt() are both method calls on existing objects. Today we write our own.

sections.title: Pieces of a method declaration
rows: How the method can be accessed — covered fully in Static vs Instance Methods
The type of value this method sends back
How we refer to and call this method
Input values the method needs to do its job

sections.text: We are only using static here as a fixed pattern, without explaining it — it's covered properly soon in Static vs Instance Methods. For now, every method we write inside Main will use static, matching main() itself.

sections.title: Tracing square(5)
steps: main() calls square(5) — control jumps into square's body, with number set to 5
return 25 sends 25 back to wherever square was called from
Control returns to main(), and result is set to 25

sections.text: A method is declared inside a class, but outside any other method — notice square() sits alongside main(), not inside it. We haven't formally learned classes yet (that's the Object-Oriented Programming module), but for now, every method in this course lives directly inside the Main class, next to main().

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Declaring one method's body inside another method's braces — methods must be siblings inside the class, never nested inside each other.
Forgetting that calling a method requires parentheses, even with no arguments — square not square() does not call it.
Mismatching the return type — declaring int square(...) but trying to return a String, or forgetting to return anything at all.
Assuming a method runs automatically just because it's declared — it only runs when explicitly called.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a method in Java?
questions.answer: A named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task and can be called from elsewhere in the program, avoiding the need to duplicate the same logic.

questions.question: What are the main pieces of a method declaration?
questions.answer: A return type, a name, a parameter list (possibly empty), and a body containing the code that runs when the method is called.

questions.question: What happens when a method is called?
questions.answer: Control jumps into the method's body, running its code with the given argument values assigned to its parameters, and (if it returns something) sends a value back to the calling code before control returns there.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A method is a named, reusable block of code — Java's version of a function — declared with a return type, a name, a parameter list, and a body. Calling a method transfers control into its body with the given arguments assigned to its parameters; if it has a non-void return type, it sends a value back via return before control returns to the caller. Methods let us avoid duplicating the same logic across a program by giving it one name we can call wherever it's needed.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: A method is a mini-program with a name. Declare once, call as many times as you like — each call is a fresh trip into the body and back.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What are the four main pieces of a method declaration?
What happens to control flow when a method is called?
Can a method be declared inside another method's body?
